perturbation.original_text: (b) The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not use the Confidential Information to (i) divert or attempt to divert any known business or customer of the Company or (ii) solicit for employment, or initiate contact for employment with, any known employee of the Company; provided, however, nothing will prohibit: (i) recruiting efforts that are not based on confidential information or general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting employees of the Company and the hiring as a result, (ii) the solicitation and hiring of any individual who is no longer employed by the Company at the time of such solicitation or hiring and (iii) the hiring by you of anyone who initiates contact with you regarding such employment.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of six (6) months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not use the Confidential Information to (i) divert or attempt to divert any known business or customer of the Company or (ii) solicit for employment, or initiate contact for employment with, any known employee of the Company; provided, however, nothing will prohibit: (i) recruiting efforts that are not based on confidential information or general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not specifically targeting employees of the Company and the hiring as a result, (ii) the solicitation and hiring of any individual who is no longer employed by the Company at the time of such solicitation or hiring and (iii) the hiring by you of anyone who initiates contact with you regarding such employment.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-compete period to 6 months may be deemed unreasonable under New York law, especially considering the nature of the confidential information. This creates a potential conflict with what a court would deem a reasonable restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Labor Law § 201-d restricts employers from engaging in certain discriminatory practices based on an employee's or potential employee's legal recreational activities outside of work. While this section doesn't directly address non-compete agreements, the enforceability of such agreements is scrutinized under New York law for reasonableness, including the duration. A six-month restriction may be viewed as insufficient to protect legitimate business interests, especially concerning highly confidential information, thus potentially undermining the company's ability to protect its business under applicable legal standards.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a one-year non-compete period, which was reduced to six months in the changed text. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that a six-month restriction may be deemed unreasonable under New York law, especially considering confidential information, potentially undermining the company's ability to protect its business. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address non-compete agreements, it references New York Labor Law, which the Law Explanation ties to the reasonableness requirement for such agreements. This reduction in time directly impacts the enforceability of the non-compete agreement, potentially rendering it insufficient to protect the company's interests under applicable legal standards, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Expenses . In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction issues a final, non-appealable judgment, then the non-prevailing party shall be liable and pay to the prevailing party the reasonable legal fees and expenses such prevailing party has incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Expenses . In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, the non-prevailing party shall be liable and pay to the prevailing party the legal fees and expenses such prevailing party has incurred in connection with such litigation, regardless of any final judgement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a 'final, non-appealable judgment' before awarding legal fees could contradict the American Rule, which generally states that each party bears its own legal fees unless a statute or contract provides otherwise.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. v. Wilderness Society, 421 U.S. 240 (1975)
perturbation.law_citation: Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. v. Wilderness Society, 421 U.S. 240 (1975)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the 'American Rule' as established in Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. v. Wilderness Society. The American Rule generally requires each party to bear its own attorney's fees unless explicitly authorized by statute or contract. By removing the requirement for a 'final, non-appealable judgment,' the contract implies that legal fees could be awarded prematurely, even if the case is still under appeal, thus conflicting with the principle that fee shifting typically occurs only after a conclusive resolution.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. | Supreme Court | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. Supreme Court 421 U.S. 240 95 S.Ct. 1612 44 L.Ed.2d 141 ALYESKA PIPELINE SERVICE COMPANY, Petitioner, v. The WILDERNESS SOCIETY et al. No. 73—1977. Argued Jan. 22, 1975. Decided May 12, 1975. Syllabus Under the 'America Rule' that attorneys' fees are not ordinarily recoverable by the prevailing litigant in federal litigation in the absence of statutory authorization, respondents, which had instituted litigation to prevent issuance of Government permits required for construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline, cannot recover attorneys' fees from petitioner based on the 'private attorney general' approach erroneously approved by the Court of Appeals, since only Congress, not the courts, can authorize such an exception to the American rule. Pp. 247-271. 161 U.S.App.D.C. 446, 495 F.2d 1026 , reversed. Robert E. Jordan, III, Washington, D.C., for petitioner. Dennis M. Flannery, Washington, D.C., for respondents. Mr. Justice WHITE delivered the opinion of the Court. 1 This litigation was initiated by respondents Wilderness Society, Environmental Defense Fund, Inc., and Friends of the Earth in an attempt to prevent the issuance of permits by the Secretary of the Interior which were required for the construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline. The Court of Appeals awarded attorneys' fees to respondents against petitioner Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. based upon the court's equitable powers and the theory that respondents were entitled to fees because they were performing the services of a 'private attorney general.' Certiorari was granted, 419 U.S. 823 , 95 S.Ct. 39, 42 L.Ed.2d 47 (1974), to determine whether this award of attorneys' fees was appropriate. We reverse. 2 * A major oil field was discovered in the North Slope of Alaska in 1968. 1 In June 1969, the oil companies constituting the consortium owning Alyeska 2 submitted an application to the Department of the Interior for rights-of-way for a pipeline that would transport oil from the North Slope across land in Alaska owned by the United States, 3 a major part of the transport system which would carry the oil to its ultimate markets in the lower 48 States. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for a 'final, non-appealable judgment' before the non-prevailing party is liable for legal fees. This directly contradicts the American Rule, which generally requires each party to bear its own legal fees unless explicitly authorized by statute or contract. The scraped snippets from Alyeska Pipeline Svc. Co. v. Wilderness Soc'y, 421 U.S. 240 (1975) state that 'attorneys' fees are not ordinarily recoverable by the prevailing litigant in federal litigation in the absence of statutory authorization'. By eliminating the 'final, non-appealable judgment' requirement, the contract now potentially allows for fee shifting before a final resolution, conflicting with the American Rule.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return or destroy, at its sole option, all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 6, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, any return or destruction is subject to law, regulation and internal document retention policies.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Return of Confidential Information. Upon any request of the Company, the Receiving Party will destroy all copies of the Confidential Information, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information. Notwithstanding the destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 6, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, any return or destruction is subject to law, regulation and internal document retention policies.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'return' option and mandating destruction of all confidential information, regardless of its nature, may contradict legal requirements for retaining certain records, especially if the information pertains to regulatory compliance or potential litigation. For example, certain financial records need to be retained for a specific period.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification to mandate destruction of all confidential information potentially contradicts the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX). While SOX primarily addresses financial record-keeping, it includes provisions about the destruction or alteration of documents with the intent to impede or obstruct an investigation. If the confidential information contains financial data or records relevant to an investigation, the mandatory destruction clause could be interpreted as conflicting with SOX's requirements for maintaining accurate and accessible records.

perturbation.justification: The change removes the option to 'return' confidential information, mandating its destruction. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) if the information contains financial data relevant to an investigation. The modified clause, by mandating destruction, could impede the maintenance of accurate and accessible records required under SOX. Snippet 2, referencing 15 USC Ch. 98 (PUBLIC COMPANY ACCOUNTING REFORM AND CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY), supports that mandatory destruction could violate SOX's requirements for retaining relevant documents, creating a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 18. Termination . This Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. Termination . This Agreement shall terminate and be of no further force and effect six (6) months from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination period of the agreement to six months, may make the duration unreasonable for the protection of trade secrets. This is particularly relevant given New York's common law protections for trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Integrated Cash Management Services, Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc., 920 F.2d 171 (2d Cir. 1990)
perturbation.law_citation: Integrated Cash Management Services, Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc., 920 F.2d 171 (2d Cir. 1990)
perturbation.law_explanation: The case Integrated Cash Management Services, Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc. outlines how New York courts determine if something is a trade secret and how they determine the length of trade secret protection. By reducing the termination period, the confidentiality of data and trade secrets could be compromised because a shorter time frame could be ruled as unreasonable for protecting the business.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a one-year termination period, while the changed contract reduces it to six months. According to the LLM's Law Explanation, this could potentially compromise the confidentiality of data and trade secrets, because a shorter time frame could be ruled as unreasonable for protecting the business. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, indicating that the reduced term might be unreasonable for trade secret protection. While there are no Scraped Law Snippets to directly assess this, the fact that the explanation is rooted in the case Integrated Cash Management Services, Inc. v. Digital Transactions, Inc., and New York common law protections for trade secrets, the shorter duration presents a situation where the business is no longer able to protect it's trade secrets.
